summary:
The short sharp pain from a tooth is quickly sent to the brain via A-delta fibers, which transmit rapid sensory information through well-myelinated nerve pathways. This type of fiber allows for immediate and precise communication between oral tissue damage and central nervous system processing centers. \boxed{A}

The summary captures both core aspects requested—how such pain travels swiftly along specific neural channels—and focuses directly on explaining why these particular nerves carry this kind of acute sensation efficiently. It also maintains brevity while providing essential context about what makes Aδ-fibers uniquely suited for transmitting brief but intense stimuli associated with dental issues. This type of fiber allows for immediate and precise communication between oral tissue damage and central nervous system processing centers. \boxed{A}
